scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: Cognitive accessibility. [pause] The widest area of need — and the hardest to address systematically.

scenes.name: Range
scenes.text: It covers a wide range. [pause] Dyslexia. Dyscalculia. ADHD. Autism spectrum conditions. General processing differences. [pause] And students without any diagnosis who simply find a particular text too dense.

scenes.name: Transformations
scenes.text: AI works through <emphasis>text transformation</emphasis>. [pause] Simplify language. Restructure into tables and steps. Add concrete examples and analogies. [pause] The meaning stays. The cognitive load drops.

scenes.name: Prompts
scenes.text: Specific prompts work. Vague ones do not. [pause] Rewrite at this reading level. Convert to a numbered list of single actions. Make a three-column table with concept, explanation, example.

scenes.name: By condition
scenes.text: Different conditions benefit from different adaptations. [pause] Dyslexia — shorter sentences, dual channel with audio. ADHD — structured sections, bullet summaries. Autism — explicit, systematic formats.

scenes.name: Principle
scenes.text: The principle to hold onto. [pause] Cognitive accessibility is not about lowering standards. [pause] The learning objective stays the same. Only the path to understanding changes.

scenes.name: Limits
scenes.text: Teacher judgement remains essential. [pause] AI simplifies as much as you ask — it cannot tell whether the result still meets the curriculum. [pause] Set the boundary. Preserve the key terms. Review the output.

scenes.name: For all
scenes.text: And remember — this benefits every student. [pause] Anyone can find a particular text confusing in a particular moment. [pause] Adapting content becomes a normal classroom skill, not a special accommodation.
